title: Brunch Upgrade
description: Extend checkout and host a Sunday brunch.
content: Brunch Upgrade Highlights Optional Sunday Brunch Upgrade lets you host a farewell brunch at Rixey Manor. Great for extending celebration and easing the “post-wedding blues.” Works best if more than 12–14 guests are attending; smaller groups may just use included continental breakfast. Options: hire a caterer, DIY/family-style, or go off-site . Brunch Upgrade extends Sunday checkout from 10am → 1pm . Spaces: Front porch, back patio, dining room, or ballroom depending on size and weather. Menu ideas include bagels, fruit, yogurt, eggs, bacon, pastries, and mimosa stations. Communicate plans to guests in advance (casual attire expected). Fee covers extra hours/staffing; food/catering is separate. Typically runs 10am–12pm , with cleanup by 1pm. Extending the Celebration After the big day, a post-wedding brunch is a wonderful way to continue celebrating and spend extra time with family and friends. At Rixey Manor, we offer an optional Sunday Brunch Upgrade , giving you relaxed time to say farewells before everyone heads home. Feeding a Crowd vs. Intimate Brunch If you only have 12–14 overnight guests , our included continental breakfast (bagels, yogurt, fruit, coffee) may be plenty. If you want to invite more guests (extended family, bridal party staying off-site), then a dedicated brunch upgrade is wise . This requires extra planning for food, seating, and setup. Catered Brunch or DIY Hire a Caterer Carpe Donut (a local favorite) can provide donuts, coffee, and light breakfast. Other local cafes/caterers can offer buffets with eggs, bacon, fruit, etc. Caterer option = less stress; they handle food and setup while you relax. We coordinate their arrival and any rentals (chafing dishes, etc.). DIY/Family-Style Families sometimes prefer to cook together or set out bagels, pastries, and fruit. Kitchen available, but coordinate if heavy cooking—must be cleaned afterward. Keep DIY simple (pastries, fruit, casseroles). Only choose DIY if you have helpers lined up—you’ll be tired post-wedding. Go Off-Site Skip on-site brunch and gather at a local restaurant (e.g., Country Café in Culpeper). This option avoids cleanup and doesn’t require the upgrade. Extended Checkout Standard Sunday checkout = 10am . With brunch upgrade = 1pm checkout . Allows time to eat, mingle, and enjoy without rushing. We adjust your contract to reflect extended hours. Space and Setup Front Porch or Back Patio → fresh-air brunch if weather is nice. Dining Room → seats ~14, with foyer/library for overflow. Ballroom → can host 30+ for large brunch gatherings. Linens: we reuse wedding linens if Monday pickup; if not, we use in-house stock. Menu Ideas Bagels or pastries with cream cheese/jam. Fresh fruit platter or salad. Yogurt and granola. Egg dishes (quiche, frittata, scrambled eggs). Breakfast meats (bacon, sausage). Mimosa station with leftover bubbly and OJ. Coffee/tea (we brew larger urns if needed). Sweet treats: leftover cake or donuts. Carpe Donut option: fresh cinnamon sugar donuts (needs outdoor power hookup). Communication If inviting beyond overnight guests, tell them in advance (website or insert). Mention casual attire is fine—everyone will be relaxed and possibly hungover. Costs Brunch upgrade fee covers venue access and staff until 1pm. Food costs are separate (catering or groceries). Pros of Brunch Softens the “wedding blues” (see Wedding Blues Support). Lets you reminisce, say goodbyes, and enjoy food you probably missed at the reception. Creates a meaningful final chapter to the weekend. Time & Flow Brunch typically runs 10am–12pm . Coffee ready by 10am; food ~10:15–10:30am. Departures usually begin around noon. By 1pm : check-out, final walkthrough, keys returned. Clean-Up Caterers handle their own cleanup. DIY brunch → assign helpers to tidy. We handle trash removal. Couples must still gather belongings from rooms by checkout. Closing Brunch is the relaxed, cozy epilogue to your wedding. Whether catered or casual, it allows you to wind down with loved ones, laugh about dance floor antics, and share donuts in sweatpants before heading home.

title: Extra Meals
description: Ideas for vendor meals, Friday dinners, or Saturday lunches.
content: Extra Meals Highlights Weddings = multiple meals: rehearsal dinner, wedding-day breakfast/lunch, late-night snacks, brunch. Rixey provides continental breakfast (fruit, yogurt, bagels, coffee) for on-site guests. Always plan lunch on wedding day —don’t rely on mimosas alone! Snack baskets provided; let us know personal favorites if desired. Accommodate dietary needs with notice (gluten-free, vegan, etc.). Avoid overloading with excess food—fridge space is limited. Late-night snacks: s’mores at firepit, sliders, pizza, donuts, hot chocolate station . Feed vendors too (photographers, DJs, planners). Friday Rehearsal Dinner Plan food for rehearsal/welcome gathering (BBQ, food trucks, restaurant dinner). Confirm arrangements during Final Walkthrough . Remember staffing may be needed for bar service etc. Saturday Breakfast Provided by Rixey: fruit, yogurt, bagels, juice, coffee. Self-serve and casual (fits with hair/makeup schedules). Can accommodate dietary requests with notice (GF bagels, almond milk, etc.). Snacks Throughout Snack baskets stocked (granola bars, chips). Many snacks go untouched—don’t overpack. Wedding Day Lunch Critical to avoid fainting during vows! Options: sandwiches, wraps, or deli platters. Avoid messy or stain-prone foods. Feed vendors on-site (hair/makeup, crew). We’ll double-check during Final Walkthrough that lunch is covered. Dietary Needs Notify us of special diets (gluten-free, diabetic, vegan). Kitchen available for medical or baby-related needs (e.g., milk storage). Late-Night Snacks S’mores Provided fire pit and some skewers. Couples must bring marshmallows, grahams, chocolate, extras. Note: nut options must be labeled. Messy, so kept outside. Other Options Sliders, pizza delivery, donut wall. Caterers may offer late-night snack add-ons. Cold-weather: hot chocolate machine (3 gallons included; bring more chocolate milk if extended). Vendors All vendors on-site over a meal should be fed a meal. Most vendor contract specify that they should be fed the same meal as guests. Photography teams, DJ, and one bartender typically get fed as soon as the newlyweds eat. Post-Wedding Leftovers Leftover desserts, dinners, or snacks can be reused for brunch or after-party. Coordinate donations if feasible. Summary Plan for all meals of the weekend , not just the wedding dinner. A fed wedding party = a happy wedding party.

title: What time can flowers be dropped off?
content:
Floral can be dropped off anytime after 8am. 
Make sure in your floral contract the florist isn’t responsible for arbor or reception set up.
All floral must be there upon your photographers arrival to capture detail photos.

title: Delivery
content: We recommend tent delivery on Thursdays and any other deliveries (not coming through the same company as the tent) on friday so they have somewhere to store it and we don't have to move things multiple times. The tent can be taken down on Sunday or Monday - but Monday is preferred.

title: Cottage & Overnight Suite Details
description: Details about the newlywed suite, cottage, and overnight accommodation options.
content:
Cottage & Overnight Suite Details

Newlywed Suite: Available for either your wedding night (Saturday) or Friday night — your choice. Some couples switch to Friday night if they want to leave Saturday evening after the reception. Just let us know if your preference changes.

Cottage: Available for overnight guests or as a staging/storage area. The back bedroom has a queen-sized bed. During back-to-back wedding weekends, the cottage can be used for Sunday pickup staging.

Family/Guest Rooms: Additional rooms in the manor can be booked for family members who want to stay on-site. Payment via Venmo or check — coordinate directly with Isadora.

What's provided: Bedrooms have sheets and bedding. We also have extra linens, towels, and bedding in case of spills or if someone forgets to pack. If someone has a stringent diet or is breastfeeding, they can use the fridge and microwave.

title: Guest Accommodations
description: Hotel blocks, Manor bedrooms, and cottage information.
content: Guest Accommodations Make sure your guests are armed with the info they need to arrive and lodge comfortably: Nearest Airports: Washington Dulles International (approximately 1 hour away) and Charlottesville-Albemarle Airport (approximately 45 minutes away) are the closest major airports. Train Station: Culpeper has an Amtrak train station (on the Boston–New Orleans line) for guests who prefer to travel by rail. Hotels: The closest hotels are in Culpeper (about 15–20 minutes from the Manor). Popular choices include the Holiday Inn Express, Hampton Inn, and Wyndham Inn. We recommend blocking rooms there early if you have many out-of-town guests. Transportation Note: Rixey Manor is in a rural area – Uber, Lyft, and local taxis are not reliable here . Encourage guests to use your shuttles (see Shuttle Planning ) or carpool. By providing this information on your wedding website or in save-the-dates, you’ll help guests plan their travel and feel taken care of.

title: Guest Comfort Tips
description: Ensuring guests are hydrated, comfortable, and cared for.
content: Guest Comfort Tips We want your guests to be comfortable and have a great time from start to finish. A few simple considerations can go a long way in enhancing guest experience: Hydration is Key: Ensure water is readily available to guests throughout the day. For the seated dinner, have water glasses at each place setting and don’t cut off water service – it keeps the tables looking elegant and helps prevent anyone from overindulging on alcohol. Also consider having water or lemonade dispensers during cocktail hour, especially on hot days. Hydrated guests = happy guests. Welcome Refreshments: It’s a lovely surprise for guests to be greeted with a refreshment upon arrival. On hot summer days, think pre-ceremony lemonade or iced tea on the front porch as guests walk in. In cooler weather, a warm cider or hot chocolate station before the ceremony can be a cozy touch. We can set this up with your caterer. It immediately makes guests feel cared for and comfortable as they wait for the ceremony to begin. Adequate Staffing: This might not seem like a “guest comfort” item at first, but having enough service staff absolutely affects guest experience. For example, at least 6 catering staff for a 100-person wedding is recommended so that tables are served and cleared promptly. When staffing is sufficient, guests never have an empty water glass, dirty plate left in front of them, or a long line at the bar. Talk to your caterer (or us) about the staffing plan – it’s worth ensuring they’re bringing enough people so that service is smooth and your guests feel attended to. We can help advise on this based on your guest count and meal style. Comfort Items (Weather Dependent): Think about the forecast and time of year. For summer weddings, provide things like program fans, parasols, or sunscreen for an outdoor ceremony. In winter or fall, consider space heaters, lap blankets, or wraps for an outdoor cocktail hour. Even a basket of inexpensive flip-flops near the dance floor for tired feet can be a hit. While these specific ideas weren’t in your documents, they’re small touches you can choose to add if budget allows. Overall, put yourself in your guests’ shoes: a little shade, a little warmth, a drink in hand, and attentive service. Those are the ingredients for a comfortable guest experience. We’ll work with you and your vendors to achieve this effortlessly.

description: Ensuring guests are hydrated, comfortable, and cared for.
content: Guest Comfort Tips We want your guests to be comfortable and have a great time from start to finish. A few simple considerations can go a long way in enhancing guest experience: Hydration is Key: Ensure water is readily available to guests throughout the day. For the seated dinner, have water glasses at each place setting and don’t cut off water service – it keeps the tables looking elegant and helps prevent anyone from overindulging on alcohol. Also consider having water or lemonade dispensers during cocktail hour, especially on hot days. Hydrated guests = happy guests. Welcome Refreshments: It’s a lovely surprise for guests to be greeted with a refreshment upon arrival. On hot summer days, think pre-ceremony lemonade or iced tea on the front porch as guests walk in. In cooler weather, a warm cider or hot chocolate station before the ceremony can be a cozy touch. We can set this up with your caterer. It immediately makes guests feel cared for and comfortable as they wait for the ceremony to begin. Adequate Staffing: This might not seem like a “guest comfort” item at first, but having enough service staff absolutely affects guest experience. For example, at least 6 catering staff for a 100-person wedding is recommended so that tables are served and cleared promptly. When staffing is sufficient, guests never have an empty water glass, dirty plate left in front of them, or a long line at the bar. Talk to your caterer (or us) about the staffing plan – it’s worth ensuring they’re bringing enough people so that service is smooth and your guests feel attended to. We can help advise on this based on your guest count and meal style. Comfort Items (Weather Dependent): Think about the forecast and time of year. For summer weddings, provide things like program fans, parasols, or sunscreen for an outdoor ceremony. In winter or fall, consider space heaters, lap blankets, or wraps for an outdoor cocktail hour. Even a basket of inexpensive flip-flops near the dance floor for tired feet can be a hit. While these specific ideas weren’t in your documents, they’re small touches you can choose to add if budget allows. Overall, put yourself in your guests’ shoes: a little shade, a little warmth, a drink in hand, and attentive service. Those are the ingredients for a comfortable guest experience. We’ll work with you and your vendors to achieve this effortlessly.

title: Pet Allergy Accommodations
description: Accommodations for guests with severe pet allergies.
content:
Pet Allergy Accommodations

If you have guests with severe pet allergies (especially to dogs), let us know in advance. We can keep the dogs away from the specific building where the allergic guest will be staying or spending time.

Just confirm which room or building the guest will be using so we can redirect the dogs accordingly. The main house, cottage, and outdoor areas can all be managed separately.

title: Welcome Bag Ideas
description: Suggestions for creating memorable welcome bags.
content: Welcome Bag Ideas If you have a lot of guests staying at local hotels, welcome bags are a wonderful touch to greet them. You can ask the hotel to hand these out at check-in. Some fun ideas to include: Snacks & Water: Traveling is thirsty work! Include a couple of bottled waters and easy snacks. For a local flair, you could do Virginia peanuts, Route 11 chips (a VA brand), or candies from a Culpeper sweet shop. Guests will appreciate a late-night nibble or something for the room. Itinerary & Contact Info: A one-page printout that lists the weekend events (rehearsal, ceremony, brunch – whatever they’re invited to) with timings and addresses. Also include important contacts (your planner or a designated friend’s phone #, in case of questions), shuttle pickup times if applicable, and maybe a weather forecast. This answers a lot of questions guests might have. Area Info or Souvenir: Since many are new to the area, you could include a little guide to local attractions (we can provide a pamphlet for Culpeper tourism, for example). A map of the town or a list of nearby restaurants for free time is nice. Some couples also throw in a cute Virginia souvenir – like a postcard, a small jar of local jam or honey, or a sticker that says “Virginia is for Lovers.” Even a custom door hanger for their hotel room that says “Do Not Disturb – Recovering from [Your Last Name] Wedding” can be a cute keepsake! Personal Touch: Write a short welcome note to your guests, thanking them for making the trip. It can be in the form of a printed letter or a handwritten note. Doesn’t have to be individual for each guest – even a general one that starts with “Dear Friends and Family, Thank you for being here to celebrate with us…” adds a warm, personal welcome. Sign it from you both. People really do read those and feel the love. Emergency Kit Items: Toss in a couple of Advil packets or Pepto-Bismol chewables – a humorous nod to “wedding recovery.” Your guests might find it funny and useful the next morning. Assembling welcome bags can be done a week or two before the wedding. It’s a fun bridesmaids project! Remember to coordinate with the hotel about delivery (some hotels will place them in rooms or give at check-in – ask if there’s a fee for that service). Keep it simple; you don’t need to spend a ton on these. It truly is the thought that counts. Even just water, snacks, and a note will make guests smile.

title: Shuttle Planning
description: Tips for timing, scheduling, and booking shuttles for guests.
content: Shuttle Planning Having shuttles for your guests is highly recommended at Rixey Manor. Here are some tips to plan it effectively: Understand Shuttle Timing & Charges: Shuttle companies typically charge by the hour. Your time is counted from the first pickup to the final drop-off back at the hotel. It’s not just “drive time” – if a shuttle waits idle for an hour, that hour counts. Keep this in mind when scheduling, to avoid paying for unnecessary hours. Where possible avoid having guests arrive too early - it shortens the morning and leads to and earlier hair and makeup start, as well as creates a less relaxed pre-ceremony atmosphere if the house is full of people. Schedule Multiple Runs Thoughtfully: Most couples do two shuttle runs at the end of the night. We suggest scheduling the first shuttle departure 30 minutes after the dessert is served , and the second (last) shuttle about 15 minutes after the last dance . This staggers the departures. Alternatively, if you have two shuttles and want everyone gone around the same time, you can send both shuttles out about 15 minutes after music stops. Guests always take longer to gather their belongings and say farewells than expected, so that buffer prevents you from running into overtime charges. (We’ve seen folks get hit with an extra hour charge because the shuttle had to wait for stragglers – our schedule hack avoids that.) Communicate the Schedule Clearly: Publish the shuttle schedule for your guests. Post it on your wedding website, include it in any welcome bag itineraries , and email it to the shuttle company a week prior to double-check it works on their end. A typical note might be: “Shuttles will depart Hotel X at 3:30 PM and 4:15 PM to bring guests to the Manor. Return shuttles at 8:45 PM and 10:00 PM back to Hotel.” Make sure guests staying elsewhere know where to park or which hotel to get to for pickup. No Uber – Plan Rides! As noted, you can’t count on ride-shares out here in the countryside. Please let guests know Uber/Lyft won’t be available at the end of the night. If someone isn’t on the shuttle and shouldn’t drive, they’ll need a designated driver. We require that any guest who has been drinking must take a shuttle or be driven by a sober driver. It’s for everyone’s safety. We will absolutely help coordinate if someone gets stuck – but it’s better to plan ahead so no one is stranded or tempted to drive. With smart scheduling and clear communication, your guests will appreciate a smooth, safe ride back to their hotels. And you can party stress-free knowing everyone will get home OK.

content: Buying your table linens. They come creased, and getting them pressed can be as expensive as renting them. Pressing them yourself is an arduous, time-consuming experience; what will you do with 10+ linens at home? Dry cleaning/pressing is about $14 per linen. Negotiate with a rental company and see what they can do for you.

title: Don't be afraid to ask questions — a great caterer will guide you through options.
content: Make sure your caterer's quote includes sufficient staff for setup, serving, and cleanup. At minimum, about six staff are needed for events over 70 people. If your caterer is providing a plated meal, they should also have dedicated servers. For buffet style, staff are still needed to monitor and replenish. If the caterer doesn't include enough helpers (or if you're DIY-ing some food), you can arrange additional staff through Rixey (see Rixey Staffing Guide). Understaffing slows down dinner and leaves messes, so it's critical to have enough hands on deck.

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: S'mores by the Fire (Fall/Winter): If you're having a wedding in cooler weather, s'mores are an incredibly popular late-night treat at Rixey Manor. We have portable fire pit setups for this. Just note: s'mores are messy! We require that they be done outdoors (usually on the back patio) and we'll use a dark tablecloth. You'll need to bring the supplies (marshmallows, chocolate bars or fun fillings, graham crackers, and long roasting sticks). Weather permitting, a s'mores station adds a fun, interactive touch

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: Hot Chocolate Bar (Winter): For winter weddings, our hot chocolate machine is a hit. We will typically have it running before the ceremony (about 3 gallons worth). If you'd like the hot chocolate available all night, we ask that you bring an additional 3 gallons of chocolate milk to refill the machine. We stock basics like whipped cream and some flavored syrups. You can also provide fun add-ins – think mini marshmallows, peppermint sticks, or even mini bottles of Bailey's, Kahlua, or bourbon to spike the cocoa

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: For a grand finale or a fun twist, consider adding some "sprinkles" of excitement to your celebration:

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: Sparkler Exit: One of the most popular and stunning ways to end the night at Rixey Manor is with a sparkler send-off. We do allow it, but only certain types: please purchase the wedding-safe sparklers (these are usually long, smokeless sparklers specifically made for events). We have the process down to a science. Longer sparklers (36-inch) are best, because they burn for over 2 minutes, giving your photographer plenty of time to capture the moment. We usually do the sparkler exit toward the end of the reception (after last call), so plan it as a true "exit"

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: Confetti or Petal Toss: Disolvable in water biodegradable confetti or real petals can be used outdoors. Dried lavender buds are another eco-friendly option that also smell lovely. Avoid paper or foil confetti

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: Champagne Pop: A fun photo-op is a champagne spray – popping a bottle and spraying it in celebration (best done outdoors, obviously!). We've seen hilarious and epic shots of a groom popping champagne with his groomsmen

title: Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable.
content: Seasonal Welcome Drinks: In spring/summer, welcome guests with refreshing lemonade or iced tea. In fall/winter (October through April), set up a warm drink station – think hot apple cider, hot chocolate, or mulled wine

title: Face and hair products (we have them but your wedding day isn't the time to try a new brand)
content: We maintain our own "oh no!" supply closet. So if you don't have something, ask us before running to a store. We typically have: pain relievers, bandages, deodorant, toothbrushes & toothpaste, mouthwash, floss, tampons/pads, baby wipes, lint rollers, super glue, safety pins, sewing kit, white chalk (great for covering a stain on a white dress in a pinch), earring backs, Q-tips, tweezers, nail clippers, bug spray, sunscreen, hand warmers, umbrellas, phone chargers, extension cords, duct tape, and even spare votive candles, lighters, scissors, and vases for decor needs.

title: Face and hair products (we have them but your wedding day isn't the time to try a new brand)

For decor, pack a few spare place cards, a spare favor if they're personalized, and maybe a few extra invitations/programs for keepsakes or if one gets damaged. We often have blank cards and paper for emergency printing or hand-written signage.

title: On that subject, if there is someone you want to book. Book them as soon as you have a date.
content: Don't put your guest list together and then try to figure out how you will pay for that many meals or fit all of the guests into your venue. Have a clear idea of what you would like your food budget to be per head, where you would like to do your ceremony, and your budget in general (invitations aren't cheap). Then, come up with a maximum number of guests. It will help to figure out some guest list rules (that apply to both sides) to get you to that number. The first thing we always tell couples is if they wouldn't take someone out for a three-course meal when it isn't their wedding, why would they do that on a day that is so much more than that? Wedding parties are hard to change. Start small, choose wisely, then increase the guest list total as the months roll on.

title: Please try to limit the number of shot only liquors (Tequila).
content: Having a signature cocktail is a fun way to personalize your bar menu. It can be as simple as a renaming of a classic drink, or a completely unique concoction. A few tips and ideas:

title: Please try to limit the number of shot only liquors (Tequila).
content: Keep It Simple: Choose a drink that's easy for bartenders to make in bulk. For example, a specialty spritzer or punch can be pre-mixed in dispensers. Avoid anything with more than 3 ingredients or requiring blending/muddling per drink. Think along the lines of a flavored lemonade + spirit, a classic old-fashioned with a twist, or a champagne cocktail.

title: Please try to limit the number of shot only liquors (Tequila).
content: Use Your Favorites: Did you two bond over bourbon? Consider an Old Fashioned or a whiskey sour as your signature. Love the beach? Maybe a Mojito or a Mai Tai. The key is it should be something you actually enjoy.

title: Please try to limit the number of shot only liquors (Tequila).
content: Match the Season or Theme: For a fall wedding, something like an Apple Cider Moscow Mule fits perfectly. In winter, a Spiced Rum Punch could work. Spring might be a Lavender Lemonade with gin, and summer could be a Watermelon Mojito or Blueberry Lemonade Vodka.

title: Please try to limit the number of shot only liquors (Tequila).
content: Non-Alcoholic Version: It's thoughtful to have a non-alcoholic "signature mocktail" as well, so non-drinkers can participate.

title: Please try to limit the number of shot only liquors (Tequila).
content: Signage and Story: If you do a signature drink, make a little sign for the bar that lists the ingredients and maybe a one-liner about why you chose it. Guests get a kick out of the why.

content: This is less about what is a good thing to spend your money on and more about what, in our humble opinion, is not! And also a little education about where to start on the big-ticket items.

title: Skimping on the candles in the ballroom. Candles are a beautiful accent that is cost-effective.
content: For most couples, we are the first stop on the wedding trail. It is hard, if not impossible, to book any other aspect until you have a venue since booking the venue also gives you a date! From this point onward, you are faced with a massive list of tasks broken down into months and when you need to achieve them. We hate this and would like to propose our way of organizing your wedding planning based on some simple points:

title: Skimping on the candles in the ballroom. Candles are a beautiful accent that is cost-effective.
content: Throw out the month-by-month timeline. There is nothing wrong with booking things as soon as you want to book or buy them. Why you should wait until a month or two out to buy your guest book is crazy. Just get things as you find them, and ensure you have them all before the wedding. You can get most last-minute things on Amazon (overnighted) or Michaels. If you care about it, buy it early, and if you don't care about it, just buy it the next time you drive by a wedding-type store and be done with it.

title: Skimping on the candles in the ballroom. Candles are a beautiful accent that is cost-effective.
content: DIY Flowers. Sometimes, this can work out. Take your own time into consideration. Building flower arrangements can take 3-5 people for a whole day. Ensure you get a florist's quote and practice arranging before making this decision. If you are DIY'ing flowers, please have a clear plan and as much pre-prep done before arriving at the Manor on Friday. On Saturday, there is no time, space, or patience to make those decisions.

content: Little things get forgotten more often than big ones. Make sure you've packed: cake knife & server, toasting flutes, memorial table photos, rings and ring box, marriage license, vow books, pens for the guest book, extra stationery.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Pro tip: assign one trusted person (Maid of Honor, Best Man, or sibling) to safeguard the truly vital items until Saturday morning— rings, license, vows — so you don't have to think about them.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Keep your overnight bags and personal items separate from décor boxes. Clothes, toiletries, and medications should go in luggage — not in a décor bin.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Please schedule any week before drop-offs as early as possible so we can build our week around them.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: We recommend buying all the alcohol, sodas, etc., and dropping them off on the same trip – it saves you a lot of loading and unloading.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: We usually ask if all alcohol gets stacked in the bar room. We will unpack and set up anything to do with the bar to see if anything is missing.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Most other early drop-off items get put in the Dining Room until your official setup begins on Friday.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Move-In Day—usually the Friday before your wedding—is when the excitement really kicks in. It's the day you arrive at Rixey Manor with your family or wedding party, and all those décor items you so carefully packed.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: We'll coordinate with you on what time you can arrive on Friday. In most cases, that is 3pm. We'll have the Manor clean and ready for you. When you get here, we'll do a quick check-in, show you to your rooms, and give you a brief tour. We'll also review the weekend schedule one more time.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: After checking in, it's time to unload any remaining items you brought. If you did an early drop-off earlier in the week, much of your stuff might already be here.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: On the day you 'move in,' we recommend unpacking the boxes into the rooms they will be set up in; we always leave the ceremony set up till the day of the wedding, just in case Virginia weather hits us unexpectedly.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Get comfortable in your assigned suite/room. Hang up the wedding dress and bridesmaid gowns (we have plenty of hangers and garment racks, and a steamer if dresses need de-wrinkling).

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: Typically, Friday late afternoon or early evening we'll hold your ceremony rehearsal. Usually this is about 4pm or 5pm. Gather the key people—wedding party, parents if they're part of processional, and of course your officiant. We'll go to the ceremony site and do a walk-through of the processional and recessional. This rehearsal is important for calming nerves and ensuring everyone knows what to do on the big day.

title: Sweetheart Table Box: toasting flutes, signage, candles
content: After the rehearsal, it's time to celebrate! Most couples host a rehearsal dinner or welcome gathering on Friday evening. At Rixey, you have options:

content: The typical wedding alcohol calculation is one drink per person per hour, so on average eight drinks per person, so 800, for a 120-person wedding. Don't take out the people you know who don't drink, etc; they balance out the people who drink two drinks an hour.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: All Rixey staff get paid $350 per person in 2026. We will Venmo request you for the payment at the final walkthrough.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: You will need staffing. The food trucks only provide staffing to serve the food. You will still need a team to set the tables, clear them, help guests, restocking bathrooms, etc. We can provide them through the Manor (we recommend an Event Captain and then a second person per 30 people) - we also have a full-service event staff that we can recommend if you would like a more formal team of servers.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Ensure any caterer or food truck you hire is properly licensed (with a health permit) and carries liability insurance. We may ask for a Certificate of Insurance in advance.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Timeline-wise, we front load all the official things - introductions, dances, and speeches all happen before we release your guests to dinner, so there isn't a strict time for eating, and guests can drift to the trucks whenever they want.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Pre-Ceremony Welcome Drink: Offering lemonade, iced tea, or fruit-infused water as guests arrive (or hot cocoa/apple cider in cooler months) is a thoughtful touch. We can set up a beverage station on the porch or patio for this.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Menu Choices: Too many entrée options can complicate service. Two entrées plus a vegetarian option is usually plenty. Also, consider what's in season — ingredients are fresher and often cheaper. If cultural dishes are important, discuss them early so the caterer can plan.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Real China vs. Disposables: Real dinnerware adds elegance but comes with rental and staffing costs. High-quality disposables can be a budget-friendly alternative. Choose what fits your vibe and budget.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Food truck weddings run slightly differently than fully catered weddings. They're a fun, casual option we love hosting.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: For up to 120 guests, we recommend three dinner trucks and one dessert truck. We also recommend that at least one truck can provide cocktail hour food (usually stationed apps on one or two tables) and one wood-fired pizza truck, as they generally serve several pies at once, making a good grab-and-go option for guests.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Limit options at each truck, and put menus on the tables so that guests can choose their truck and menu selection before they are standing in line.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Consider buying disposable plate/party sets from Amazon (you can get them delivered directly here), and then all the food is served on cohesive plates.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: You need a separate rental company to provide linens, napkins, etc. We can discuss what quantities you need.

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:

title: Through our experience, we've learned a few tricks that make a big difference:
content: Keep Water on Tables: Even for buffet meals, ask the caterer to place water carafes and glasses at each table. It elevates the table setting and keeps guests hydrated (preventing overindulgence). Similarly, don't cut coffee/tea service after cake — many guests love a hot drink with dessert.

title: Weddings where the ceremony is being held earlier in the day
content: Pinterest can be overwhelming for brides and grooms who are new to wedding planning. It can trigger three main emotions: overwhelming panic, inferiority, and second-guessing. To avoid feeling this way, it's best to take a step back and answer a few key questions on paper before diving into Pinterest. Think about how you want your wedding to feel, what elements are important to you, and what you want your wedding to say about you as a couple. Then, create specific boards for each element of your wedding, such as wedding flowers, boutonnieres, and table plans. This will help you stay organized and avoid feeling overwhelmed. Keep in mind that many of the images you see on Pinterest are staged by vendors and may not be realistic for your budget or timeline. It's important not to second-guess yourself and stick with your decisions once you've made them.

title: Weddings where the ceremony is being held earlier in the day
content: First looks allow for a much quicker photo session as everyone is focused and not worrying about the bar opening and appetizers being served during cocktail hour. It also allows you two to join your guests at cocktail hour.

title: Weddings where the ceremony is being held earlier in the day
content: Please schedule the last shuttle 15 minutes before it needs to depart - drunk people take a while to move, and someone has always lost their phone or bag.

title: Weddings where the ceremony is being held earlier in the day
content: Receiving lines, while traditional, take forever. Please budget 30 seconds per guest at minimum (100 guests equals 50 minutes). We suggest not doing these.

title: Weddings where the ceremony is being held earlier in the day
content: Consider one more thing – one of the most popular things among the couples who have married here before - eating dinner in the library or bridal suite. The guests don't miss you when they're eating, and 20 minutes alone together is invaluable.

title: Weddings where the ceremony is being held earlier in the day
content: Front-load everything as much as possible in the timeline – finish dinner, do the speeches, cut the cake. It makes the job much harder for the DJ if you keep starting and stopping the party. Also, people are held wedding hostages till you cut the cake, and it's nice for the older generations to head out if they don't want to stay for the party.

title: Bud Vases
description: Bud vases available for borrowing for table decor and centerpieces.
content:
Bud Vases

We have a collection of bud vases available for you to use for table decor and centerpieces. These work beautifully with real flowers for a simple, elegant look.

Best approach: Plan to assemble them on Friday (day before the wedding). Grace will be on-site Friday evening for the rehearsal and is happy to help work on them. If they don't all get done during rehearsal, your bridal party can finish up later that evening.

We can also set aside extra bud vases for side tables, cocktail tables, or other surfaces where you might want a little floral touch. Just let us know how many you need.

title: Décor Borrowing List
description: List of items available to borrow from Rixey (cake stands, table numbers, votives).
content: Décor Borrowing List We are proud to offer a library of décor items that you can use for free! Rixey Manor has accumulated a lot of goodies over time, and we keep an updated inventory on our website for you to browse. Before you rent or buy something, check our Décor Borrowing List – you might find we already have it. If there’s an item you were hoping for that’s not on our list, let us know; we’re always taking suggestions for useful pieces to acquire. A few notes on borrowing décor from us: How to Browse & Reserve: We’ll send you the link to our inventory list. It includes things like various signs (welcome sign, bar menus, etc.), easels, card box, cake stands, table numbers, lanterns, drapery, candle holders, and even some furniture. If you see something you’d like to use, just tell us and we’ll set it aside for your weekend. It’s first-come-first-serve, but since we only do one wedding per weekend, it’s virtually always available to you. We’ll confirm at your final walkthrough which items you want to borrow, and our team will have them ready. Small Items We Have: To give you an idea, here are a few small items you do not need to bring because we have them on-site: snack baskets , irons & steamers for steaming dresses/suits, votive candle holders (a bunch of clear glass ones), basic glass vases , scissors and lighters , etc.. We keep these on hand so you don’t have to remember things like “who has a lighter for the unity candle?” or “did anyone bring scissors to cut the cake ribbons?”. Just ask us and we’ll provide. Larger Decor Items: We also have some bigger decor pieces available. For example, Rixey Manor owns three ceremony arbors : a white birchwood arch, a lightweight circular metal arbor, and a wooden hexagon arbor. Many couples use these for their ceremonies (you can decorate them with flowers or fabric). If it’s on the list, you can use it. This can save you hundreds in rental costs. Condition and Care: All borrowed items are provided as-is . They might have minor wear (which usually isn’t noticeable to guests). Please treat them kindly so we can continue offering them. Our staff will handle the setup of any large items (like moving an arbor into place). For smaller decor you borrow (say a sign or lantern), you or your decorator can set it up wherever you like, or ask us for help. At the end of the night, our team will collect our items – you don’t need to pack them up, but please don’t accidentally pack our items with your stuff! We’ll do a sweep to make sure we retrieve everything. Always Ask: If you’re looking for something specific – chalkboard signs, gold easels, a donut wall, etc. – ask us if we have it or can get it. Worst case, we don’t; best case, we do (or we know someone local who can lend it). We love expanding our collection based on what couples actually want to use, so your feedback is welcome. Using the Décor Borrowing List is a win-win: less expense and hassle for you, and it makes us happy to see our decor getting loved! We’ll go over the inventory during planning meetings to remind you what’s available. There’s a little bit of everything, so definitely take advantage.

title: Welcome Mirror
description: Welcome mirror available for borrowing, with calligraphy by Grace.
content:
Welcome Mirror

We have a welcome mirror available for couples to borrow for their wedding. Grace can do the lettering/calligraphy with your names, date, or welcome message — no problem at all, just give us a heads up.

The mirror is typically placed at the entrance area — front porch for a front-of-house wedding, or near the ceremony entrance for a garden/tent setup. We'll figure out the best spot based on your layout.

title: Signature Drink Ideas
description: Custom cocktail and specialty drink ideas to personalize your menu.
content: Signature Drink Ideas Having a signature cocktail is a fun way to personalize your bar menu. It can be as simple as a renaming of a classic drink, or a completely unique concoction. A few tips and ideas: Keep It Simple: Choose a drink that’s easy for bartenders to make in bulk. For example, a specialty spritzer or punch can be pre-mixed in dispensers. If you name it something cute (e.g., “Marry-Me Margarita” or “Happily Ever After Highball”), guests will enjoy ordering it. But our advice: avoid anything with more than 3 ingredients or requiring blending/muddling per drink (it could slow down bar service). Think along the lines of a flavored lemonade + spirit, a classic old-fashioned with a twist, or a champagne cocktail. Use Your Favorites: Did you two bond over bourbon? Consider an Old Fashioned or a whiskey sour as your signature. Love the beach? Maybe a Mojito or a Mai Tai reminiscent of vacations. Wine lovers? Perhaps a seasonal sangria as the signature drink. The key is it should be something you actually enjoy – because you’ll be the ones front and center with those drinks in hand! Match the Season or Theme: For a fall wedding, something like an Apple Cider Moscow Mule (apple cider, vodka, ginger beer) fits perfectly – call it “Apple of My Eye Mule.” In winter, a Spiced Rum Punch or spiked hot cocoa (if doing a welcome drink) could double as a signature. Spring might be a Lavender Lemonade with gin (pretty and floral), and summer could be a Watermelon Mojito or Blueberry Lemonade Vodka . These seasonal flavors delight guests and can tie into your color scheme too (garnish with fruit that matches your colors!). Non-Alcoholic Version: It’s thoughtful to have a non-alcoholic “signature mocktail” as well, so non-drinkers or younger folks can participate. Essentially this can be the same base drink without the alcohol. For instance, your “Blushing Bride Sangria” can have a non-alcoholic version with sparkling cider instead of wine. Just let the bartenders know the recipe for both versions. We can put a little sign that says “Also available alcohol-free” so everyone feels included. Signage and Story: If you do a signature drink, make a little sign for the bar that lists the ingredients (so people know what they’re getting) and maybe a one-liner about why you chose it. E.g., “The Aviation – the cocktail we both ordered on our first date! A mix of gin, maraschino liqueur, violet, and lemon.” Or “The Bulldog – named after our beloved pup. A spiked Arnold Palmer (sweet tea + lemon)!” Guests get a kick out of the why. And our bartenders get a heads-up on what to prepare in advance. If you need ideas, our bartending team has seen a lot – feel free to ask and we can suggest popular ones. Signature drinks are by no means required, but they do make for great photos and a personalized touch. Plus, if you limit it to one or two options, it can simplify your bar stock needs. Cheers to that!

title: Signature Drink Ideas
description: Custom cocktail and specialty drink ideas to personalize your menu.
content: Signature Drink Ideas Having a signature cocktail is a fun way to personalize your bar menu. It can be as simple as a renaming of a classic drink, or a completely unique concoction. A few tips and ideas: Keep It Simple: Choose a drink that’s easy for bartenders to make in bulk. For example, a specialty spritzer or punch can be pre-mixed in dispensers. If you name it something cute (e.g., “Marry-Me Margarita” or “Happily Ever After Highball”), guests will enjoy ordering it. But our advice: avoid anything with more than 3 ingredients or requiring blending/muddling per drink (it could slow down bar service). Think along the lines of a flavored lemonade + spirit, a classic old-fashioned with a twist, or a champagne cocktail. Use Your Favorites: Did you two bond over bourbon? Consider an Old Fashioned or a whiskey sour as your signature. Love the beach? Maybe a Mojito or a Mai Tai reminiscent of vacations. Wine lovers? Perhaps a seasonal sangria as the signature drink. The key is it should be something you actually enjoy – because you’ll be the ones front and center with those drinks in hand! Match the Season or Theme: For a fall wedding, something like an Apple Cider Moscow Mule (apple cider, vodka, ginger beer) fits perfectly – call it “Apple of My Eye Mule.” In winter, a Spiced Rum Punch or spiked hot cocoa (if doing a welcome drink) could double as a signature. Spring might be a Lavender Lemonade with gin (pretty and floral), and summer could be a Watermelon Mojito or Blueberry Lemonade Vodka . These seasonal flavors delight guests and can tie into your color scheme too (garnish with fruit that matches your colors!). Non-Alcoholic Version: It’s thoughtful to have a non-alcoholic “signature mocktail” as well, so non-drinkers or younger folks can participate. Essentially this can be the same base drink without the alcohol. For instance, your “Blushing Bride Sangria” can have a non-alcoholic version with sparkling cider instead of wine. Just let the bartenders know the recipe for both versions. We can put a little sign that says “Also available alcohol-free” so everyone feels included. Signage and Story: If you do a signature drink, make a little sign for the bar that lists the ingredients (so people know what they’re getting) and maybe a one-liner about why you chose it. E.g., “The Aviation – the cocktail we both ordered on our first date! A mix of gin, maraschino liqueur, violet, and lemon.” Or “The Bulldog – named after our beloved pup. A spiked Arnold Palmer (sweet tea + lemon)!” Guests get a kick out of the why. And our bartenders get a heads-up on what to prepare in advance. If you need ideas, our bartending team has seen a lot – feel free to ask and we can suggest popular ones. Signature drinks are by no means required, but they do make for great photos and a personalized touch. Plus, if you limit it to one or two options, it can simplify your bar stock needs. Cheers to that!

title: Sprinkles
description: Guidance on just some of the little extras we have seen
content: Sprinkles For a grand finale or a fun twist, consider adding some “sprinkles” of excitement to your celebration: Sparkler Exit: One of the most popular and stunning ways to end the night at Rixey Manor is with a sparkler send-off . We do allow it, but only certain types: please purchase the wedding-safe sparklers (these are usually long, smokeless sparklers specifically made for events). We have the process down to a science – our staff will coordinate lighting and lineup, so you get that perfect sparkling tunnel to run through. No need for you to get buckets or lighters; we’ll handle all that. A few tips: longer sparklers (36-inch) are best, because they burn for over 2 minutes, giving your photographer plenty of time to capture the moment. And we usually do the sparkler exit toward the end of the reception (after last call), so plan it as a true “exit” where you two are leaving and guests are seeing you off. It makes for magical photos and an unforgettable end to the night. Confetti or Petal Toss: Some couples opt for a celebratory toss at the ceremony conclusion or exit. As noted, disolvable in water biodegradable confetti or real petals can be used outdoors. For example, if you want guests to toss petals as you walk back up the aisle married, that’s allowed (and looks gorgeous in photos). Dried lavender buds are another eco-friendly option that also smell lovely. Avoid paper or foil confetti – those are not allowed since they’re not easily cleaned up. Tip: If doing a toss, have the officiant make a quick announcement or have it noted in the program so guests know to grab the confetti cones or bags before you come down the aisle. Champagne Pop: A fun photo-op is a champagne spray – popping a bottle and spraying it in celebration (best done outdoors, obviously!). If you want to do this, coordinate with your photographer. We’ve seen hilarious and epic shots of a groom popping champagne with his groomsmen or the couple spraying it together as the bridal party cheers. We’ll provide a broom after for any corks or spills. Just use real champagne (or cava) for a good spray – sweet wines or prosecco can be sticky. And maybe don’t aim at your faces… we’ve also seen champagne-in-eye incidents! The idea of “sprinkles” is to add a little extra flair. These are by no means required, but if you’ve dreamt of that iconic sparkler photo or confetti raining down, we’re here to make it happen safely and smoothly. Just remember to buy those long sparklers in advance (enough for about half your guest count is usually sufficient, since not everyone grabs one, but we suggest one per guest to be safe). Store them in a cool, dry place until the wedding. When the time comes, we’ll line everyone up, pass out sparklers, and use our torches to get them lit quickly. Then it’s your time to shine! Run (or dance) through the sparkle tunnel , kiss in the middle if you want an epic photo, and soak in that exhilarating moment – it’s like the grand finale of the best show of your life.

title: Pinterest Board Guidance
description: Tips on creating useful Pinterest boards without overwhelm.
content: Pinterest Board Guidance Pinterest is an amazing tool for inspiration, but it can also become overwhelming during wedding planning. We recommend a strategic approach: Define Your Vision First (Off Pinterest): Before you even open Pinterest, take a step back and jot down a vision . How do you want your wedding to feel ? What are the three most important elements to you? What vibe or story do you want it to convey about you as a couple? By answering these questions first (on paper), you’ll have a guiding light and won’t get lost in the sea of images. Organize by Category: Create a few specific boards for key aspects of your wedding – for example: Flowers , Bridal Party Attire , Centerpieces , Cake Ideas , Photo Poses , etc. This way, when you pin ideas, they’re sorted and easier to reference later. It prevents “inspiration overload” on one giant board. Remember Pinterest ≠ Reality: Many images on Pinterest are from styled shoots or luxury budgets – in other words, they’re staged and often not realistic for a normal wedding timeline or budget. Don’t feel “inferior” if your own wedding can’t have a thousand imported candles in a field or a five-tier designer cake on a mountain top! Take inspiration from those images, but adjust expectations knowing your wedding will be beautiful in its own way . Trust Your Decisions: Once you’ve chosen something – your color scheme, your dress, your centerpieces – stop second-guessing yourself. It’s tempting to keep scrolling and find new ideas, but that often leads to doubt or decision fatigue. Pin your favorite ideas, make the decision, and then move on to the next task. Your choices will come together into a lovely, unique day. By using Pinterest intentionally, you can gather ideas without drowning in them. And of course, feel free to share your Pinterest boards with us! We’re happy to look through them to understand your vision (just don’t be surprised if we remind you to keep it practical).

title: Seasonal Touches
content: Seasonal Touches Embrace the season of your wedding – it can make the celebration even more memorable. Here are some seasonal touches to consider: Seasonal Welcome Drinks: Offering a drink that matches the weather is always delightful. In spring/summer, welcome guests with refreshing lemonade or iced tea as they arrive. In fall/winter (October through April), set up a warm drink station – think hot apple cider, hot chocolate, or mulled wine . Guests love warming up with a cozy drink during cooler outdoor moments. These little seasonal details also photograph well and add to the atmosphere. S’mores by the Fire (Fall/Winter): If you’re having a wedding in cooler weather, s’mores are an incredibly popular late-night treat at Rixey Manor. We have portable fire pit setups for this. Just note: s’mores are messy! We require that they be done outdoors (usually on the back patio) and we’ll use a dark tablecloth for the s’mores table to hide the mess. You’ll need to bring the supplies (marshmallows, chocolate bars or fun fillings like peanut butter cups or caramel squares, graham crackers, and long roasting sticks). If you have guests with nut allergies, let us know so we can put out a little sign to caution them about cross-contamination. Weather permitting, a s’mores station adds a fun, interactive touch – just be aware that if it’s very windy or rainy, we might not be able to light the fire. Hot Chocolate Bar (Winter): For winter weddings, our hot chocolate machine is a hit. We will typically have it running before the ceremony (about 3 gallons worth of hot cocoa). If you’d like the hot chocolate available all night, we ask that you bring an additional 3 gallons of chocolate milk to refill the machine. We stock basics like whipped cream and some flavored syrups. You can also provide fun add-ins – think mini marshmallows, peppermint sticks, or even mini bottles of Bailey’s, Kahlua, or bourbon to spike the cocoa. A spiked hot chocolate bar can be a fabulous seasonal treat for adult guests! Seasonal Florals & Decor: Use what’s in season – e.g., pumpkins, mums, and rich foliage in fall; lush greenery and berries in winter; cherry blossoms or tulips in spring; sunflowers or lavender in summer. Seasonal decor not only tends to be more cost-effective, it also creates an immersive atmosphere. For instance, one couple provided cozy pashminas in autumn colors for guests to wrap up in during a chilly October ceremony – they doubled as favors. Another had a sparkler send-off for a New Year’s Eve wedding which fit the festive winter vibe. We’re happy to brainstorm seasonal ideas that fit your theme. Seasonal touches like these make the wedding feel thoughtful and immersive. They’re the kind of details guests remember (“They had hot cider waiting for us – it was wonderful!”). Plus, leaning into the season often saves money, as you’re using what’s readily available. Let’s make the most of the time of year in your plans!

title: Alcohol & Bar Setup
description: How to stock your bar (quantities, sample menus, specialty bars).
content: Alcohol & Bar Setup Highlights You can supply your own alcohol at Rixey—big savings and full control of brands. Planning baseline: ~1 drink per person per hour (better to overbuy; unopened wine/spirits are often returnable—check your retailer). Three service levels: Beer & Wine Only ; Beer, Wine & 2 Signature Cocktails (batched) ; Modified Full Bar (core liquors) . Detailed quantities for ~100 guests included below (wine by the case, beer by sixtel/quarter kegs or by the case, liquor by handle). We have a dedicated Bar Room , and standard bar tools. Avoid half kegs —unsafe/heavy on the Manor staircase; use ¼ and ⅙ kegs only. Send us your draft shopping list a few days before you buy—we’ll sanity-check quantities. We have limited storage so we require all beer is served on tap, and limited storage for canned seltzer etc. Overall Consumption Guideline A safe rule of thumb is 1 drink per person per hour . Example: 8 hours × 100 guests ≈ 800 drinks across beer, wine, and cocktails. Some won’t drink; others will have more—it balances out. Overestimate slightly: unopened wine and liquor can often be returned (confirm store policy). Running out at 10pm is a bummer; a couple extra cases are not. Bar Package Types 1) Beer & Wine Only (often plus bubbly for toasts) For ~100 guests (whole weekend): Wine (~8 cases total; 12 bottles/case) Sparkling: ~2 case (toasts/mimosas) White/Rosé: ~4 cases (more in summer) Red: ~2 cases (more in winter) 8 cases is generous and likely more than enough for Fri–Sun; returns cover excess. Beer Prefer small kegs (stay cold, cost-effective): ~2 sixtels (~5.2 gal each) + 2 quarter-barrels ≈ ~2 full kegs in volume. Crowd >120 or beer-heavy? Add another sixtel or two. No half-barrels —too risky on the stairs. Styles: a light lager/ale + a darker/craft option in the quarters, plus a couple popular sixtels. Non-Alcoholic Don’t forget sodas and water —see Logistics for amounts. (Even beer/wine bars need NA options and soda for simple mixed requests.) 2) Beer, Wine & Specialty Cocktails (very popular) Keep beer/wine similar to above (you may trim wine a bit for a cocktail-forward crowd). Add two pre-batched signature cocktails (His/Hers; seasonal is great). Quantity : plan ≥2 gallons per cocktail (~256 oz = ~30–40 servings depending on pour size). For 100 guests, bartenders mix ~3–4 total gallons across two recipes if your crowd loves cocktails. Batch-friendly picks : spiked lemonade, sangria, mojito punch, margaritas (summer), bourbon-spiced apple cider (fall). Have the garnishes and specialty mixers for those recipes ready (mint, lime wheels, cinnamon sticks, jalapeños, etc.). 3) Modified Full Bar (beer, wine, and core liquors) Wine : ~6 cases (slightly less since many will choose mixed drinks) ; distribute types like above. Beer : similar to Beer & Wine plan ( ~4 small kegs or equivalent cans). Liquor (handles / 1.75L) Vodka 1–2 Gin 1–2 Rum 1–2 Jack Daniel’s 2–3 (very popular) Fireball 1–2 (shot-popular) Optional nicer bourbon 1 Tequila : consider limiting to ~1 handle (shot-heavy; potentially skip to curb straight shots). Mixers & Accoutrements Sodas : Coke, Diet, Sprite, ginger ale, tonic , club soda Juices : orange, cranberry, pineapple, sour mix Other : grenadine, lime juice, bitters Garnishes : lemons, limes, cherries, olives Specialty Bars (Optional Upgrades) Add a focused station to any base (usually needs extra staffing ): Whiskey/Bourbon Tasting Bar (curated labels, neat/rocks) Rum Bar (pairs with a cigar area) Champagne/Mimosa/Bellini Bar (fruit purees; great for cocktail hour) Logistics & Other Supplies Ice ~60–80 lbs per 100 guests (for chilling and serving). We have an ice freezer—err on the side of more. Water ≥6 cases of small bottles (8–12 oz reduces waste) = ~144 bottles . We’ll also set ice-water dispensers ; bottles are great for pockets, welcome bags, and late-night hydration. Sodas (12-packs unless noted) ~4 cases Cola , ~2 cases Diet , ~2 cases lemon-lime , ~2 cases ginger ale , ~2 cases club soda/tonic (gin drinkers want tonic; club is universal). Specialty mixers if your menu uses them: ginger beer (Mules), Red Bull (if requested)— ~24 cans each . Juices & Mix Ingredients Orange juice 3–4 gal , cranberry ~2 gal , pineapple ~1 gal , sour mix ~1 gal . Simple syrup and lime juice for common builds. Garnishes & Condiments 2 bags lemons/limes (we’ll cut). Mint , jalapeños (spicy margs), olives , maraschino cherries . Margarita salt , optional colored sugar rims, Tabasco for Bloody Mary lovers. Barware & Drinkware We provide standard bar tools (shakers, strainers, wine keys, dispensers). We provide sturdy disposables (plan counts for wine, beer, cocktails, water, and champagne flutes), napkins, straws. Coordinate delivery/pickup if renting. Leftover Plan Unopened wine/liquor are often returnable ; beer usually isn’t . Soda may not be returnable, but we are happy to hold on to extras. We can store leftovers short-term if you’re traveling Sunday—please coordinate. No ½ Kegs Reiterating: no half-barrels —unsafe on the historic stair to the bar. Stick to ¼ and ⅙ . Bar Setup at Rixey Primary service is in our dedicated Bar Room ; cocktail hour often flows onto the adjacent patio (or remains indoors if preferred). We can stage a satellite bar on the front lawn for cocktail hour or arrivals. We’ll finalize layouts, menus, and staffing at the Final Walkthrough based on your timeline and spaces. Check with Us Before Buying A few days before your big store run, send us your draft list . We’ll adjust quantities based on what we have in stock. With a well-planned bar, service is fast and festive—and everyone stays safe (please pair bars with shuttles/transport planning). Cheers to a great celebration! 🥂

title: Bar Taps & Drink Dispensers
description: Details on bar tap heads and drink dispensers available at the venue.
content:
Bar Taps & Drink Dispensers

Bar taps: We have 3 tap heads at the bar. Current house kegs include Angry Orchard, Blue Moon, and Miller Lite. Couples can bring their own keg to add to the lineup (e.g., Shocktop).

You don't need to bring buckets or taps for the kegs — anything OTHER than the kegs themselves is provided.

Drink dispensers: We have 5 drink dispensers available for your water/drink table. Great for infused water, lemonade, iced tea, or any non-alcoholic beverages. No need to rent or buy your own.

title: Bedrooms
content: Rooms Highlights Standard rental: 4 Manor bedrooms + Cottage , Friday–Sunday. Total capacity: ~12–14 comfortably, ~18 with air mattresses. Thursday arrival or Sunday “staycation” available for extra fee. No single-room rentals; venue is exclusive-use. Kids and pets allowed with planning. House amenities: garment racks, irons, steamers. Checkout: strip linens, consolidate belongings. Standard Rooms Mountain Room : Queen bed, in-room bathtub, quiet. Maple Room : King bed, largest floorspace, hall bath. Great for airbeds or cribs. Back Room : Queen bed, private staircase, ensuite shower. Newlywed Suite : King bed, large suite (typically used by couple). Cottage : Sleeps 3–4 upstairs, sofa bed downstairs, kitchen, half bath down. Sleeping Capacity 12–14 in beds. Up to 18 with airbeds (we have some; bring linens for extras). Maple Room and Cottage are best for extra beds. Room Add-On Options Air Mattresses/Cots : allowed in Maple/Mountain/Cottage. Extra Nights : Thursday check-in or Sunday night stay (availability dependent). Day-Use Rooms : non-overnight guests can use rooms for getting ready. Sunday Staycation : couple remains after everyone leaves = private mini-moon. Usage Tips Allocate rooms by need (kids, older guests, party groups). Vendors or childcare staff can use extra beds if available. Use Maple Room or Newlywed Suite for bridal party prep (biggest floorspace). Cribs welcome Pets Dogs allowed by request. Checkout Strip used beds, put linens/towels in utility room. Pick up bedroom trash and bring all dishes and glasswear to the kitchen. Large Groups For >18, use local hotels for overflow. Guests can collect cars next morning. Safety & Belongings Rooms don’t have individual locks; valuables can be stored with us if needed. Safe available for marriage license, rings, or cas h. Summary Treat Rixey like your home for the weekend. Add extra nights if you want a slower pace, or use every corner creatively (kids’ playroom, groomsmen game night).

title: Bar Upgrades
description: Options for specialty, or champagne welcome bars.
content: Bar Upgrades Highlights Upgrades elevate the guest experience: champagne welcomes , outdoor specialty bar , table-side wine/champagne , cigar corner , late-night cocoa/coffee spikes . Most upgrades require extra staff (bartender/server/barback) to keep the main bar flowing and lines short. Real glassware slows service - we suggest limiting real glassware to tables and potentially cocktail hour. Remember we include hard clear plastic glasses in your package. Hydration & safety are baked in: visible water/coffee, mindful last call, bartenders monitor over-service. Upgrade Menu (with Staffing Notes) Champagne Welcome Drink Tray-passed bubbly (or a welcome cocktail) at ceremony arrival or start of cocktail hour. Plan ~1 glass per adult ; begin pouring 20–30 minutes prior. Staffing: dedicated server/bartender - may be doubled as an extra hand. Budget-smart: prosecco or cava in place of Champagne. Specialty Bar (Cocktail Hour) Signature Cocktails, or specialty drinks tasting served on the front lawn after the ceremony. Keep menu simple . Staffing: an extra bartender. Table-Side Wine/Champagne Service Pre-pour at seats for dinner/toasts, with roaming refills. Elegant but labor-intensive —plan ~2 staff dedicated to pours (adjust for guest count). Ensure sufficient wine/champagne inventory to match generous service. Real Glassware Everywhere Upscale feel; adds collection/washing cycles and breakage risk . Staffing: add a barback (or extra bartender). Popular hybrid: glass through dinner , switch to plastic after ~7pm. Personalized Bar Décor Custom neon, framed menus, theme props (tiki/speakeasy), couple photos. We’ll place décor securely and visibly so guests don’t miss it. Cigar Bar (Outdoors) Designated patio area with ashtrays; optional custom matchbooks/cutters as favors. No extra staff unless hiring a cigar roller. We’ll mark a safe smoking area and provide sand buckets. Late-Night Drink Upgrades Hot chocolate in winter (we stock 3 gallons pre-ceremony if cold; for all-night service bring 3 more gallons chocolate milk ). Irish coffee bar or coffee with liqueurs in the last hour. Can be self-serve or briefly staffed. Safety First Upgrades can boost consumption. Our bartenders: Watch for over-intoxication , limit straight liquor when needed. Keep water and coffee prominent all night. Do not do a last call , then pivot to water/soda to wind down. Bottom line: Upgrades are an experience investment. Pair them with the right staffing so everything feels smooth, special, and safe.
